blurb: Queues, pub/sub, Kafka, delivery guarantees, idempotency.

questions.q: At-least-once delivery. Why must consumers be idempotent?
choices: A message may be delivered multiple times (on retry / rebalance)
questions.explanation: At-least-once: producer retries on timeout; consumer may crash after processing but before ack. Either re-delivers. Consumer must tolerate duplicates — typically via dedup keys or idempotent operations.

questions.q: Kafka consumer group: 12 partitions, 4 consumers. How many partitions per consumer?
questions.explanation: Kafka assigns N partitions / M consumers = 3. Key: a partition goes to AT MOST one consumer per group. Scaling beyond partition count means idle consumers — increase partitions first.

choices: Writing to DB + publishing a message atomically
questions.explanation: Dual-write: without outbox, writing to DB + Kafka can partially succeed. Outbox: write the message to an outbox table in the same DB transaction; a separate process publishes from outbox. Atomicity preserved.

questions.q: Exactly-once semantics in Kafka. Which combo enables it?
questions.explanation: Kafka transactions (0.11+) + idempotent producer (dedup on retry) + transactional-aware consumer (read_committed) provide exactly-once for Kafka-to-Kafka pipelines. Across-system still needs app-level idempotency.

questions.q: Pub/sub vs queue semantics. If 3 subscribers follow topic X, how many see each published message?
questions.explanation: Pub/sub (fan-out): each subscriber gets every message. Queue (load-balanced): only one consumer per message. Kafka is effectively pub/sub (each consumer group is a separate topic tap).

questions.q: Dead-letter queue (DLQ): what triggers moving a message to DLQ?
questions.explanation: Message failed N (e.g., 5) consecutive times → move to DLQ for manual inspection. Without DLQ, a poison message loops forever and blocks the partition.

questions.q: What's the MAIN reason to partition a Kafka topic by entity_id?
choices: Preserve per-entity message ordering + parallelism across entities
questions.explanation: Same entity_id → same partition → same consumer thread → processed in order. Different entities → different partitions → parallel. Round-robin partitioning loses ordering.
